ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dosa entry (FOODON_00002152); no specific cheese dosa variant exists. Closest parent match used for the base dosa preparation; cheese addition is a documented variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers plain dosa. Cheese dosa is a variant with added processed cheese; no distinct FoodEx2 code exists for this fusion variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cheese dosa or dosa variants as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cheese dosa weighs approximately 160 g, representing a single medium-sized fermented rice crepe topped and folded with melted processed cheese as typically served at South Indian restaurants or street food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides nutrient data for plain dosa and individual dairy components. Cheese dosa values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN plain dosa data with USDA FoodData Central data for processed cheddar/mozzarella cheese at an estimated 25–30% cheese inclusion rate by weight. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with this blended composition. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (processed cheese entries). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific fusion preparation are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA for the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese dosa. GI of 52 is estimated using plain dosa as the analogous base food (GI ~55 per University of Sydney GI Database). The addition of processed cheese (approximately 25–30% by weight) contributes significant fat and protein, which attenuates postprandial glucose response. A downward adjustment to GI 52 is consistent with published evidence on fat-protein co-ingestion reducing glycemic response (Collier & O'Dea, Am J Clin Nutr, 1983). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 25.6) / 100 ≈ 13.3 for a 160 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from processed cheese and salted fermented batter
hypertension.guidance: At 267 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 427 mg per 160 g serving), cheese dosa contributes a significant sodium load; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request low-sodium cheese variants, and avoid frequent consumption to stay within the WHO-recommended 2000 mg/day sodium ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index attenuated by fat and protein from cheese, but caloric density is elevated
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 52 (low category) suggests a moderate postprandial glucose response; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size to one standard serving (160 g), monitor total carbohydrate intake (~25.6 g per serving), and account for the saturated fat contribution from processed cheese when managing overall metabolic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from processed cheese and trans fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 160 g serving provides approximately 6.4 g saturated fat and 0.27 g trans fat; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, choose lower-fat cheese alternatives, and ensure this dish is consumed within a diet that does not exceed the AHA-recommended 5–6% of total calories from saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential of saturated fat from processed dairy
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: High saturated fat intake from processed cheese may exacerbate systemic inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; consumption should be occasional and paired with anti-inflammatory dietary patterns rich in omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Dosa is a contemporary Indo-Western fusion dish that adapts the classic South Indian fermented rice-and-lentil crepe (dosa) by incorporating melted processed cheese — typically a blend of cheddar and mozzarella — on the inner surface before folding. The dosa batter, made from fermented rice and urad dal, is spread thin on a hot iron griddle (tawa) and cooked until crisp at the edges; grated or sliced cheese is then added to the surface, allowed to melt into a gooey, stretchy layer, and the dosa is folded or rolled. The result is a dish that combines the characteristic sour, fermented tang of traditional dosa with the rich, creamy, slightly salty flavour of melted cheese. Optional additions include chili flakes, cracked black pepper, finely chopped capsicum, and onions. Nutritionally, cheese dosa provides approximately 150 kcal per 100 g, with 6 g protein, 6.7 g fat (of which 4 g is saturated), and 16 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 267 mg per 100 g due to processed cheese and salted batter, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The estimated glycemic index of 52 places it in the low GI category, as the fat and protein from cheese slow carbohydrate digestion. It is suitable as an occasional breakfast, snack, or kids' meal but should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular risk, sodium-sensitive hypertension, or weight. It is vegetarian and widely popular in urban Indian restaurants and street food settings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cheese dosa?
Is cheese dosa good for diabetics?
Cheese dosa vs plain dosa nutrition
Can I eat cheese dosa if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from an adapted blend of ICMR-NIN plain dosa data and USDA FoodData Central processed cheese data, as no direct ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for cheese dosa as a combined dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on plain dosa GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with a documented fat-protein attenuation adjustment. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source entry for this specific fusion preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for cheese dosa as a combined preparation are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish; values would require laboratory analysis of the specific recipe. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the adapted source combination. 3. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): plain dosa batter and processed cheese contain negligible vitamin C; this is consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data for these ingredients. 4. The portion label '1 hotdog' appearing in the input data is an erroneous label from the source delivery platform and has been excluded from the output serving data; the standard serving of 160 g is used as the default. 5. Duplicate portion entries from the input (four identical '1 serving (160g)' entries) have been deduplicated. 6. Input cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source) have been replaced with values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy processed cheese entries, which are consistent with the input values and are now cited under the approved nutrient source. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for cheese dosa has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
